overview: Dredge is a combo strategy that mills its own library to populate the graveyard, then returns creatures to the battlefield to overwhelm opponents. It relies on card advantage engines and targeted disruption to protect its game plan. The deck operates independently of mana acceleration, focusing instead on graveyard recursion and board presence.
win_conditions: Overwhelming the opponent with a swarm of creatures from the graveyard
Executing a graveyard-based combo sequence to reduce opponent life
Leveraging mill effects to accelerate recursion and close the game

keep_criteria: Hands containing early mill or graveyard recursion
Hands with disruption to protect the game plan
Hands with card advantage engines to find missing pieces
Keep hands with at least two dredge creatures (Gravecrawler, Nighthowl Scavenger, etc.) or one dredge creature plus a discard outlet (Grief, Thoughtseize) and two lands.
Bazaar of Baghdad can mitigate land issues, so a hand with Bazaar + 1 land + 2 dredgers is keepable.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking interaction or graveyard synergy
Hands with only lands and non-interactive spells
Hands missing mill or recursion components
Mulligan hands with three or more lands, no dredgers, or only one land with no early plays.

early_game: Play mill spells to rapidly fill the graveyard
Cast disruption to protect early recursion attempts
Use card advantage engines to draw into combo pieces
Turn 1: Play a basic land, cast a dredge creature or discard outlet.
If opening Bazaar, play it on turn 1 or 2 to fetch a swamp and draw a card.
Turn 2: Activate dredge to put cards into the graveyard, cast additional creatures or discard outlets, and attack with available threats.
Prioritize establishing graveyard presence over hoarding cards.
mid_game: Recycle creatures from the graveyard to apply pressure
Hold targeted counterspells for opponent's threats
Maintain board presence while continuing to mill
Treat your graveyard as your primary resource.
Dredge selectively to avoid feeding opponent hate cards.
Use discard outlets to remove key opponent spells or cards.
Preserve Force of Negation for critical threats or to protect your own dredge/discard plays.
Bazaar of Baghdad should be used to fix mana or dig for answers, not just to flood the graveyard.
Commit to the board when you have sufficient card advantage and the opponent lacks sweepers or targeted removal.
late_game: Flood the battlefield with recycled creatures
Use card advantage to outlast or find answers to hate cards
Close out games with overwhelming board state
Win by overwhelming the board with a flood of dredge creatures, or by using graveyard-generated mana to cast a high-impact threat or combo finisher.
Once you have established a board presence and the opponent has no removal, commit all remaining resources to attack.
Go all-in when you have drawn into multiple dredge creatures, the opponent has no sweepers or hate in play, and your life total is safe enough to trade.
If you have Force of Negation and discard outlets ready to protect your final plays, commit aggressively.

key_cards.name: Bazaar of Baghdad
key_cards.notes: Use to draw into combo pieces or mill spells, and leverage its draw ability to find missing recursion components.

key_cards.name: Force of Negation
key_cards.notes: Hold for opponent's threats or to protect your graveyard recursion, ensuring your game plan isn't interrupted.

key_cards.name: Grief
key_cards.notes: Cast to rapidly fill the graveyard and enable dredge recursion, prioritizing it early to establish board presence.

sequencing_tips: Prioritize mill before recursion to ensure graveyard population
Hold disruption for key threats rather than using it preemptively
Use card advantage to find missing pieces before committing to recursion
Protect graveyard from hate by sequencing counterspells after opponent's interaction
Common mistakes include over-dredging into hate cards, hoarding cards instead of committing to the board, and mismanaging Force of Negation timing.
Remember that Bazaar of Baghdad can be used to fetch a swamp for black mana or a forest for green, but sequencing matters.
Always track opponent's graveyard and hand size.
Dredge is a combo-adjacent deck that wins by resource conversion, so prioritize graveyard efficiency over traditional card advantage.

matchups.advice: Out-grind with card advantage and protect your combo sequence
Mill quickly and use disruption on early threats to stabilize
Disrupt their sequence and mill to find answers before they win

Use Force of Negation to counter their counters or protect your own plays. Grief can discard their counterspells before they cast them. Dredge creatures are cheap and numerous, making them hard to counter all. Bazaar can dig for answers or additional threats.
Outpace them by establishing an early board presence. Use Grief to discard their ramp spells or key mana rocks. Dredge to generate card advantage and pressure, forcing them to answer quickly or lose.

Win by out-valuing in the graveyard and having better discard/hate resolution. Prioritize resolving Grief and Force of Negation to disrupt their dredge sequence. Sideboard in additional hate cards and discard outlets. Track their graveyard state to avoid feeding their win conditions.

common_threats: Watch for graveyard hate (Rest in Peace, Leyline, Surgical Extraction, Grafdigger's Cage) and sweepers (Wrath of God, Damnation, etc.).
Note when opponents play discard or counterspells, as these disrupt your early game.
Track if they are playing a combo that requires specific pieces, as Grief/Force of Negation can shut them down.
